System Design Copilot

A deeper system design interview prep curriculum with topic guides, building blocks, trade-offs, case studies, and interactive practice labs.

Curriculum → Case studies and interview drills

Module overview

Case studies and interview drills

Work through the full primer-inspired set of product and architecture drills, from redirect services and paste storage to finance aggregation, timelines, and ranking pipelines.

9 lessons 0/9 complete Start module

How to navigate it

Use this module like a guided drill

  • Scan the lesson summaries first so you know the progression before opening the first topic.
  • Use the “why it matters” snippets to prioritize the topics that are most likely to surface in interviews.
  • Save the case-study and practice sections for speaking answers out loud after reading the lesson once.

Objectives

What to master here

  • Practice moving from requirements and estimates to a full design quickly
  • Cover the complete set of high-signal primer case studies in one interview-ready module
  • Explain bottlenecks, scaling steps, failure modes, and operational trade-offs with confidence

How to use this module

Practice flow

  • Read the summary and explain the core trade-off in your own words.
  • Study the diagram and identify the critical path or failure boundary.
  • Answer the interview prompts aloud before marking the lesson complete.

Interview signal

What reviewers expect

  • Clear assumptions and estimated scale.
  • Justified component choices instead of pattern dumping.
  • Awareness of operations, failure modes, and trade-offs.

Lesson 1

Case study: URL shortener

Open

Design a service that creates short codes, resolves them quickly, tracks analytics, and avoids hot-key failures.

Why it matters

This case is deceptively small and is excellent practice for storage design, cache placement, and throughput reasoning.

What you will cover

Core flowScaling decisionsOperational nuances

Good interview signal

  • Explain short-code generation and collision handling.
  • Optimize the hot read path with caching.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 2

Case study: distributed web crawler

Open

Crawlers combine frontier management, politeness, deduplication, storage, and ranking across many workers.

Why it matters

This is a strong systems problem because it forces you to coordinate huge workloads safely over time.

What you will cover

Frontier and schedulingFetch and parse pipelineScale and failure handling

Good interview signal

  • Design a durable frontier and dedup pipeline.
  • Respect politeness and robots constraints.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 3

Case study: social graph service

Open

A social graph stores user relationships, supports high-volume fan-out queries, and powers many downstream ranking and recommendation systems.

Why it matters

This is a good drill for graph-shaped data, adjacency lists, caching, and consistency semantics around relationship changes.

What you will cover

Model relationships clearlyQuery pathsDownstream consumers

Good interview signal

  • Choose a graph representation based on hot queries.
  • Explain celebrity skew and cache strategy.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 4

Case study: query-result cache

Open

Design a cache that stores expensive query results while handling invalidation, skew, and freshness guarantees.

Why it matters

This is a focused practice problem for cache keys, eviction strategy, and derived-data ownership.

What you will cover

Cache the right abstractionFreshness and invalidationEviction and hot keys

Good interview signal

  • Define the cached unit and key shape.
  • State how freshness is maintained.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 5

Case study: scale-to-millions playbook

Open

Practice describing how a simple application evolves from a single box to a multi-tier, cache-heavy, autoscaled system as traffic climbs.

Why it matters

Interviewers like this because it reveals whether you can tell a coherent architectural story instead of listing disconnected components.

What you will cover

Stage architecture incrementallyKeep failure and operations in viewTell the story clearly

Good interview signal

  • Describe the architecture evolution in stages.
  • Tie each stage to a bottleneck or product requirement.
25-35 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 6

Case study: pastebin

Open

Design a paste service that stores text blobs, serves reads quickly, handles expiration, and computes analytics without slowing the user-facing path.

Why it matters

Pastebin extends URL shortening with durable content storage, object retrieval, expiration workflows, and lightweight analytics pipelines.

What you will cover

Separate metadata from contentRead path and expirationBatch analytics and cleanup

Good interview signal

  • Model the shortlink lookup separately from paste-body storage.
  • Explain expiration checks and deletion behavior.
30-40 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 7

Case study: Mint-style budgeting app

Open

Combine account linking, asynchronous transaction extraction, categorization, monthly rollups, and budget alerts in a system that handles sensitive financial data.

Why it matters

This case study combines queues, data pipelines, user-specific aggregates, and security expectations in one realistic product design.

What you will cover

Account linking and extractionCategorization and rollupsSecurity and notifications

Good interview signal

  • Make transaction extraction asynchronous and retry-aware.
  • Separate raw transactions from derived monthly spending views.
30-40 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 8

Case study: Twitter timeline and search

Open

Design tweet ingestion, fan-out, home timelines, user timelines, and search while dealing with huge read volume, celebrity skew, and asynchronous notifications.

Why it matters

Twitter is a classic interview problem because it forces you to explain fan-out trade-offs, caching, storage separation, and search indexing under extreme scale.

What you will cover

Tweet write path and fan-outTimeline readsSearch and operational skew

Good interview signal

  • Separate tweet creation from fan-out and search indexing.
  • Explain the difference between home and user timelines.
35-45 min 3 prompts 3 sections

Lesson 9

Case study: sales rank by category

Open

Compute and serve the top-selling items by category using transactional sales logs, rolling time windows, batch aggregation, and very hot read traffic.

Why it matters

This drill is great for discussing log ingestion, windowed aggregation, batch versus stream updates, and the split between analytical compute and read APIs.

What you will cover

Raw events and ranking windowsAggregation pipelineServing and freshness trade-offs

Good interview signal

  • Separate raw transaction ingest from the ranking read path.
  • Explain the rolling window and aggregation cadence clearly.
30-40 min 3 prompts 3 sections
